video_title: Hyperscalers Are Collapsing. Is the AI "Bubble" Crashing or Consolidating?
gist: Jordi Visser argues the June 2026 selloff in hyperscalers and Mag 7 is an AI 'midcycle slowdown' and rotation, not a bursting bubble: earnings are going parabolic, the buildout is real, and the trade is to be long thematic AI receivers (memory, industrials, applications) while short hyperscaler spenders on a relative basis. He sees an acute memory shortage (Micron) with no supply line-of-sight before 2028, 'loops' driving token demand and job displacement, a biotech/healthcare renaissance led by Eli Lilly, and crypto as the eventual settlement rail for agentic commerce despite being in a bear market now.

insights.claim: Jordi Visser frames the June 2026 equity action as an AI 'midcycle slowdown' — a pause inside an ongoing expansion where growth stays positive but the rate of change decelerates — not the start of a bear market or bubble burst.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Reframes the selloff as a consolidation to be bought selectively rather than a top to be shorted; argues against capitulating on AI exposure.
insights.quote: in here in macro, a midcycle slowdown is the pause inside an expansion growth is still positive. It's just that the rate of change comes down

insights.claim: The S&P 500's realized annualized return since the post-GFC March bottom (15 years) is 14.7%, and 2026's pace at the halfway mark is in line with that long-run average — undercutting the 'bubble' narrative.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A valuation/return anchor for arguing the rally is not anomalous; useful counter to bubble-callers.
insights.quote: you're talking about 14.7%. Look where we are through the halfway mark this year at the exact same pace. Yet this is called a bubble

insights.claim: The S&P 500 is up only ~7.4% year to date and that gain is driven by hyperscaler earnings going parabolic, so this is an earnings-driven bull market, not a speculation-driven one.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Distinguishes a fundamentally-supported tape from a froth top; implies the risk is earnings rolling over, not sentiment unwinding.
insights.quote: This is not a speculation driven bull market

insights.claim: Visser argues shorting the broad U.S. stock market at current forward-EPS levels has historically been a losing trade because these conditions have occurred coming out of recessions, and it is structurally hard for earnings to fall from here.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Cautions against outright market shorts; the bearish expression should be relative (spenders vs. receivers), not net short index.
insights.quote: you don't do really well when you're shorting the stock market at these points when we've been here. These have been coming out of recession periods

insights.claim: A consolidation is described as the market making an uptrend harder to own by resetting via price, time, and volatility to shake out weak hands while preserving the larger trend, with moving averages catching up to price.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Mental model for distinguishing healthy pullbacks from tops; supports holding rather than selling during the current chop.
insights.quote: a consolidation is the market's way of making a worker trend harder to own. It resets successes through some combination of price time and volatility, shaking out weak hands and preserving the larger trends

insights.claim: Hyperscalers are down ~14% month-to-date while healthcare is up ~8%, real estate up ~4%, and small caps (IWM) outperforming up ~1.5% — a broad rotation out of high-multiple Mag 7 names rather than a market-wide fall.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Sector dispersion confirms rotation thesis; signals where money is moving (defensives, small caps) versus where it is leaving.
insights.quote: Hyperscalers down 14% monthto date. This is across the board

insights.claim: Visser calls the hyperscaler decline a 'benchmark arbitrage' — a reweighting away from high-multiple names whose stocks fall even as profit margins grow and they lay off workers, because they are spending heavily with uncertain revenue timing.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Explains why fundamentally strong companies are falling; the multiple, not the earnings, is being compressed.
insights.quote: When the mega caps are going down to me, this is a a representation of what I've shown many many weeks, which is I call it the benchmark arbitrage. It's a reweing away from these high multiple names

insights.claim: Visser's preferred trade is long his thematic AI portfolio (the 'receivers' of AI spend) versus short the hyperscalers (the 'spenders') on a relative basis, noting shorting hyperscalers has worked while the receivers keep making higher highs.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Concrete pair-trade structure: capture rotation without betting against AI earnings or going net short.
insights.quote: I've told you I want to be on the short side of hyperscalers relative to the thematic AI trade

insights.claim: Multiple compression in hyperscalers reflects uncertainty over whether they can earn revenue in the door on the timeline needed while profit margins still grow, with Visser unsure they will ever hit revenue on the required timeline.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific risk being priced — revenue timing, not viability — so the trade is multiple, not solvency.
insights.quote: multiple compression is an uncertainty over their ability to get their revenues in the door while their profit margins are still growing

insights.claim: Foreign equity indices are outperforming the U.S. (Nikkei +38%, KOSPI +100%, TAIEX +53%) because they carry a higher AI benchmark weighting, while the U.S.'s AI 'spenders' fuel the move.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Suggests overseas AI-heavy indices offer cleaner exposure to the 'receiver' side than the U.S. mega caps.
insights.quote: here's the Nikkei up 38%. Here's the Cosby up 100%. Here's the TX up 53. Even Europe is equal to the US

insights.claim: High-PE names broadly (Tesla, SpaceX, Bitcoin, Palantir, gold, silver) are under pressure and unwinding as the 'debasement trade' capitulates because they lack valuations and depend on retail energy, which is being drawn into AI earnings winners instead.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Links valuation-less speculative assets to retail flow; explains simultaneous weakness across crypto/precious metals/high-beta.
insights.quote: Gold doesn't have a valuation, silver doesn't have valuation, Bitcoin doesn't have valuation, Palanteer doesn't have valuation

insights.claim: June 2026 is a major options expiration month coinciding with pension rebalancing after stocks massively outperformed bonds and AI names outperformed, which Visser cites as a flow-driven reason 'cracks' are appearing.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Technical/flow context for the timing of the selloff; implies mechanical selling rather than fundamental deterioration.
insights.quote: June is a massive expiration month... You've also got the pension rebalancings at a time where stocks have massively outperformed bonds

insights.claim: Inflation expectations via swap rates are falling and the one-year has taken out last year's lows, while 2-year and 10-year yields have given back the entire hawkish move that followed Kevin Warsh's hawkish commentary.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Argues the hawkish-Fed scare was a non-event for rates; supports ignoring macro/Fed noise when positioning in AI.
insights.quote: we are lower than we were before the evil Kevin Worsh spoke and was super hawkish. Two-year rates have given up the entire move

insights.claim: Visser dismisses fears of three Fed rate hikes, arguing that if hikes were truly coming, small caps (IWM) would not be outperforming — using cross-asset behavior to refute the hawkish macro narrative.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Tells investors to fade macro/Fed-driven AI bearishness; small-cap leadership is the tell.
insights.quote: if we were going to have Fed rate hikes, would IWM be outperforming right now?

insights.claim: AI-driven equity gains are creating net wealth that sustains consumer spending (Johnson Redbook at the highest in 25 years ex-COVID stimulus) despite no job creation and falling wages, concentrated at the higher end.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: AI is propping up consumption via the wealth effect, masking weak labor; a key macro transmission channel to watch.
insights.quote: net wealth is going up and so you don't need as much income and savings particularly when most of the spending is happening at the higher end

insights.claim: Warren Pies' chart of tech/AI capex versus residential fixed investment shows housing in decline while tech AI is in a parabolic boom, which Visser cites as why the broad public will not feel monetary tightening.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Bifurcated economy: AI capex offsets weak housing, complicating Fed read-through and feeding populist political anger.
insights.quote: Tech expenditures versus residential fixed income investment. So, housing sucks. Tech AI is in parabolic boom

insights.claim: Micron delivered a massive earnings blowout and signed a deal with Anthropic, yet its stock finished down for the week — which Visser treats as a symbol of the slowdown in the rate of change, not a fundamental problem.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Stock falling on blowout earnings confirms the consolidation/rate-of-change thesis rather than a fundamental break.
insights.quote: Micron massive blowout of earnings. You got them doing a deal with Enthropic all kinds of stuff and the stock finished down for the week

insights.claim: Micron management says it has no line of sight on when memory supply will catch up to demand, expecting only gradual industry supply improvement in 2028 despite the current year being 2026.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A multi-year memory shortage underpins sustained pricing power and the AI earnings engine; bullish for memory makers.
insights.quote: We currently do not have line of sight as to when memory supply will be able to catch up with increasing demand

insights.claim: Micron completed 16 strategic customer agreements (data center, consumer, auto) representing 20% of volume, structured as multi-year take-or-pay deals with binding volume commitments, expected to cover half or more of company revenue when completed.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Take-or-pay contracts make Micron revenue subscription-like and more predictable — a structural break from past memory cyclicality.
insights.quote: The SCAs are structured as take or pay agreements with binding commitments to purchase specific volumes over this multi-year term. I mean, isn't this like a subscription agreement?

insights.claim: Micron trades at roughly 7.5x 2027 earnings; Visser argues that even at fair value on forecasts, the likelihood of beating estimates each quarter given the trend makes the risk/reward favorable.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Low multiple plus beat-rate skew frames Micron as cheaply priced relative to the January peak optimism.
insights.quote: you're at a point now for 2027 at seven and a half times earnings

insights.claim: Visser admits selling Micron was a mistake in hindsight, having exited expecting better double opportunities elsewhere; he says if he still held it he would not be selling any more now.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Candid signal that Micron remains a hold/buy in his view despite his earlier exit.
insights.quote: if I would have been in it now, I doubt I'd be getting it rid of uh any more of it. So, that was a mistake

insights.claim: Memory demand is forecast to be amplified by future devices that carry far more memory: L2-and-above vehicle mix is more than doubling this year to over 20% (expected to exceed 40%), and humanoid robots carry roughly 10x the memory of an average car.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Quantifies a structural step-up in memory demand beyond data centers; a multi-year tailwind for memory makers.
insights.quote: Humanoid robots carry 10 times the amount of memory as an average car

insights.claim: Visser warns that once memory supply eventually catches up to demand, there will be a violent move lower in memory earnings/stocks (per a Bernstein chart not peaking until ~2027), so the multiple will be wrong at the top.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Flags the eventual cyclical downside; positioning should anticipate the second-derivative roll well ahead of the peak.
insights.quote: that's why eventually when supply does catch up demand, you're going to see a violent move lower in this as you saw in the Bernstein

insights.claim: The memory shortage is a headache even for Apple, which despite being a top supply-chain buyer is 'in the queue' for memory, with Mac Studios sold out and high-spec configurations pulled, signaling model capability outran hardware availability.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Apple hardware availability is a 'canary in the coal mine' for memory tightness and local-model feasibility.
insights.quote: if Apple, one of the most and until Nvidia, the largest and most important supply chain groups that should have been able to secure memory because of their orders for everything, they're in the queue

themes.theme: Token Demand, Models, and Cheaper-Model Risk

insights.claim: Visser believes his 'token index' has already peaked, with a best-case flatlining like 10-year yields and a worst case of deflationary token-price pressure, even as Jevons paradox keeps total compute volume exploding.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Per-unit token price may deflate while total demand surges — bullish for volume/compute even if pricing softens.
insights.quote: I really do believe that token index has already peaked

insights.claim: Roughly 60% of companies watching AI budgets are moving to cheaper models, and Visser expects most companies to eventually run open-source or cheaper models for the majority of compute (figures up to 99% cited), since the hardest work doesn't need the most expensive models.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Cheaper-model migration is a real headwind for premium-model pricing and a swing factor for token-index direction.
insights.quote: 60% of companies now watching AI budgets are moving to cheaper models

insights.claim: Visser argues mass migration to cheap Chinese open-source models (e.g., GLM 5.2) is not yet feasible because there is no affordable local hardware — Mac Minis/Studios are sold out and expensive — making Apple hardware the true canary for when token prices could collapse.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: The cheaper-model threat is gated by hardware/memory scarcity; watch Apple hardware availability as the trigger signal.
insights.quote: The problem is there are no Mac minis or Mac studios guys. There aren't any

insights.claim: GLM 5.2 is roughly 90% cheaper than Fable 5 and close to the best models, but requires expensive local hardware to avoid handing data to Chinese companies — so cost advantage doesn't translate to easy adoption.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Quantifies the open-source cost gap and the data-security/hardware friction that blunts it.
insights.quote: You're talking about a 90% cheaper model, but again, you need to go buy the hardware, and the hardware is very expensive

insights.claim: Recursive self-improvement (RSI) is now occurring — models improving their own efficiency and capability — which increases demand because better models attract more users while memory/hardware cannot keep pace.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: RSI accelerates the demand/supply imbalance in memory, reinforcing the structural shortage thesis.
insights.quote: now we have RSI happening. So we have recursive self-improvement which means the models are improving their own through the memory through the efficiency side

insights.claim: Visser cites Andrej Karpathy on Dwarkesh Patel's October interview as having been wrong by years on how soon AI agents would arrive, using it as evidence that even expert power-users underestimated the speed and thus the memory demand.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: If the best forecasters underestimated agent timing, supply planning (memory) is structurally behind demand.
insights.quote: whatever timeline people had for memory is best reflected by Andre Carpathy on Dwar Patel's interview in October... He was so wrong

insights.claim: Boris (head of Claude Code) calls agent 'loops' the next abstraction layer after agents — agents repeatedly running tasks like code review, reading feedback, and opening PRs every few minutes — and says the Opus 4.8-to-Fable jump feels as large as or larger than the leap to 4.5.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Loops are a step-change in autonomy; signals continued rapid model progress and a new workflow primitive driving token demand.
insights.quote: the jump from Opus 4.8 to fable feels as large as or larger than the leap to 4.5 which was a major leap

insights.claim: Visser argues loops are the inflection where AI begins truly replacing workers: power users can teach an agent their workflow, and if 98 of 100 workers share similar workflows, the agents do that work and those 98 are eliminated.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Loops mark the start of headcount replacement (not just hiring freezes), the next leg of margin expansion and labor disruption.
insights.quote: What loops allow the two power users to do is completely teach an agent to basically go through and do their workflow... And guess who's not going to be around? It's going to be the other 98

insights.claim: The first phase of AI's labor impact (since ~October last year) was not firing people but not hiring, producing no net job creation for ~16 months outside healthcare, with business services and information jobs negative.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Explains weak jobs data as an AI hiring-freeze effect; loops will shift this from freeze to outright replacement.
insights.quote: if you take out nurses and take out the health care side we've created no jobs over the course of the last 16 months

insights.claim: With loops, token demand no longer scales only with the number of users but with the number of recurring processes automated, so token usage 'likely explodes' as workflows run around the clock.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Reframes the token TAM from user-count to process-count — a much larger, compounding demand base underpinning AI infra.
insights.quote: If every workflow becomes a loop, token demand no longer scales only with the number of users. It scales with the number of recurring processes that you users automate

insights.claim: Visser maps loops onto quant trading, noting quant strategies are already loops (pull data, generate signals, backtest, execute, monitor risk, repeat) but require hundreds of humans inside the loop that AI can now remove, intensifying competition.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Signals rising competitive pressure and margin compression in quant/systematic trading as humans are removed from the loop.
insights.quote: quant trading is already a loop. You pull data, generate signals, you back test it, execute, monitor risk, repeat. The only difference is they need hundreds of humans sitting inside the loop. You do not

insights.claim: The 'loops' concept crystallized in June 2026 after a Peter Steinberger (OpenClaw) post passed 8 million views arguing one should design the loops that prompt coding agents rather than prompt them directly, echoed simultaneously by Boris and Karpathy.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Marks a recognizable emergence date for a new paradigm; useful for tracking when agentic-loop adoption inflected.
insights.quote: What set it off was a post by Peter Steinberger, famous of OpenClaw, which passed 8 million views. One should no longer be prompting coding agents, but designing the loops that prompt them

insights.claim: Japan announced a $2.3 trillion AI investment plan; since Japan is ~3% of global GDP, Visser extrapolates a potential ~$70 trillion global buildout, framing sovereign AI as a necessity for every country.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Sovereign AI multiplies global data-center and chip/memory demand far beyond the hyperscalers — a durable secular tailwind.
insights.quote: Japan just announced a $2.3 trillion investment plan. Japan is about 3% of global GDP. So, multiply that by three, you're up to 70 trillion

insights.claim: Visser argues memory has become a strategic asset that the government will support building, citing Trump repeatedly mentioning Micron and touting Eli Lilly's factory.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Government backstop reduces tail risk for strategic AI/memory players, supporting valuations even through cyclical stress.
insights.quote: you've reached a point where it's a strategic asset which means the government is going to be there to support building this stuff

insights.claim: Visser believes hyperscalers and frontier labs (OpenAI, Anthropic) are government necessities that will be supported and won't face doom/gloom collapse, even if their equity valuations get hit when monetization timing disappoints.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Distinguishes equity-valuation risk from existential risk; the bear case is multiple, not bankruptcy.
insights.quote: This isn't a bubble. These companies are not going out of business

insights.claim: Visser predicts Eli Lilly will become the largest company in the world within five years, driven by GLP-1s and especially next-gen 'GLP-3s' addressing both obesity and addiction at massive scale.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A concrete, high-conviction long-term single-name call anchoring the healthcare-renaissance theme.
insights.quote: Eli Liy which I've publicly said I will believe will be the largest company in the world within the next 5 years

insights.claim: Visser sees AI-driven biotech as the next massive theme, citing Demis Hassabis-style talent leaving Google DeepMind for biotech and arguing RSI brings cures close — with curing disease and extending life being a bigger macro changer than anything.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Flags AI-healthcare as a major emerging allocation theme and a defensive rotation destination amid tech volatility.
insights.quote: I think biotech is about to have a renaissance driven by AI. My instinct is we're about to cure a lot of diseases

insights.claim: Biotech consolidated through 2026 after a prior bull market and is now breaking higher with bullish 'wicks,' which Visser compares to how Eli Lilly looked when it first broke out.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Technical signal that biotech may be resuming an uptrend; a rotation beneficiary as money leaves tech.
insights.quote: now we're starting to move higher again and look at all these wicks people are still this is the way Eli Liy looked when it had broken out

insights.claim: Visser favors application-layer names trading at PEG ratios below 1 (citing Eli Lilly) as offering better Sharpe ratios in the midcycle slowdown, while warning application/cyclical names retain a commodity element in their multiples.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Stock-selection rule for the next phase: prioritize sub-1 PEG application names over hoping for 10x multibaggers.
insights.quote: If you can find names that are trading with PEG ratios below one, say Eli Liy, uh, and you can go find these, you're going to be able to have a better time

insights.claim: Visser draws from Stripe Sessions that AI-native businesses are moving from 'vibe coding' to 'vibe deploying' and that new business creation on Stripe has gone parabolic, signaling an AI-driven singularity moment for solopreneurs running global businesses with no employees.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Agentic tooling is collapsing the cost of starting and scaling businesses — a structural shift in entrepreneurship and labor.
insights.quote: AI native businesses are moving from Vibe coding, which was a year ago, to Vibe deploying

insights.claim: Visser lays out a sequence where AI agents first write/speak code, then operate infrastructure, then deploy real products, and finally transact — and the transaction step requires guardrails, which is where crypto becomes the native settlement rail.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Positions crypto as essential infrastructure for autonomous agent-to-agent commerce, a forward catalyst for the asset class.
insights.quote: First they will operate infrastructure then they deploy apps... a transact, which is the next step. So, we need to do all of this before we can get to the transaction part. For that to happen, we need the guardrails. This is where crypto shows up

insights.claim: Within Jensen Huang's 'five layer cake' framework, Visser places agentic commerce, payments, checkout, and crypto financial guardrails in layer five (the application layer), alongside Eli Lilly.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Classifies crypto as an application-layer beneficiary of the AI stack, guiding where to position as each layer unlocks.
insights.quote: Aentic commerce, payments, checkout, financial guardrails live in layer five, the application layer

insights.claim: Visser claims tokenization will unlock ~two-thirds of the planet's $700 trillion in dormant assets currently outside the global economy, calling it the largest monetary increase in human history with Bitcoin as the collateral for that world.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A massive bull case for Bitcoin as base collateral if tokenization scales; frames current weakness as a buying setup for the patient.
insights.quote: The dormant assets were releasing 2/3 of the 700 trillion on the planet, which is not part of the global economy... The collateral for that world is Bitcoin

insights.claim: Bitcoin is in a confirmed bear market (200-day moving average sloping down, making new lows) and Visser advises not playing hero — he holds plenty, trades small in/out getting stopped out, and will wait for a base rather than catch the bottom.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Tactical: avoid early bottom-fishing crypto; rotate into positive-momentum names and wait for confirmation.
insights.quote: This is a bare market. 200 days moving down. We keep going lower and lower... You do not need to step in and be a hero

insights.claim: Visser interprets the surge of bearish emails about MicroStrategy (Strategy) and Michael Saylor as a contrarian signal usually seen near a bottom, noting Strategy is essentially a Bitcoin proxy that will keep falling only if Bitcoin does.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Sentiment extreme in Strategy/Saylor bearishness may mark a crypto trough; watch as a contrarian bottoming tell.
insights.quote: Every time that I seem to get here where I get a bunch of email on strategy, that's usually near the bottom

insights.claim: Visser's 40-name equal-weight tokenized index (6 public companies, the rest tokens, across 8 verticals) did not make new lows while Bitcoin did, leading him to believe the AI-agent crypto ecosystem will lead the recovery and Bitcoin will follow.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Relative strength in the agent-ecosystem basket vs. Bitcoin is an early leadership/bottoming signal to monitor.
insights.quote: This is Bitcoin making new lows. This is the blue line which is those 40 names... it didn't make new lows, so it did break away. I believe the ecosystem needs to lead the way out and then Bitcoin will join
